title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Connecticut senator disputes Alaska claim to riverbed The Associated Press WASHINGTON (April 17, 2:00 pm ADT) - U.S. Sen. Joe Lieberman is challenging the Interior Department's decision to recognize Alaska's ownership of a riverbed in the Yukon Flats National Wildlife Refuge. Lieberman, D-Conn., wrote a letter to Interior Secretary Gale Norton in early April asking why the department overrode comments from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, which manages the refuge. The service said Alaska had not presented enough evidence to prove that the Grayling Fork, off the upper Black River, was navigable and therefore should belong to the state. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Representatives of the Bureau of Land Management said the decision was sound and based on well-established criteria. Alaska was the first state to claim a riverbed using a Bush administration policy adopted in December 2002. The administration then modified its procedures to encourage states and others to ask for "recordable disclaimers of interest" -- statements that the federal government has no ownership interest in certain properties. The disclaimers can apply not just to riverbeds but also road rights of way, including those claimed by states under the defunct federal Revised Statute 2477. The administration said the new policy would resolve legitimate property claims without lengthy court fights. summary: City bans skateboarders from park during tourist visits The Associated Press JUNEAU (April 13, 6:35 pm ADT) - The Juneau Assembly unanimously passed an ordinance that bans skateboarders from a city park when tourist buses and vans are there. The decision Monday about Marine Park Plaza came after months of discussion about conflicts between skateboarders and tourists during the summer. The ordinance also applies to roller skates, inline skates, unicycles, scooters and coasters when commercial vehicles are present. title: Panel sets groundwork for a casino
summary: JUNEAU -- The House Finance Committee on Wednesday passed a bill that could allow a gambling casino to open in Anchorage. House Bill 552, which the committee sponsored, would set up a state gambling commission with the power to allow a single casino in Anchorage. The bill was the idea of Perry Green, a well-known Anchorage furrier and poker player, who proposes to turn the failed state-owned Anchorage Seafood International plant into a casino. Green has employed some of the top lobbyists at the Capitol to push the bill, which he describes as a way to create jobs and provide tax revenue to the state and city. Green said Wednesday that he is paying for the lobbying efforts mostly from his property leasing business. He said he also has a financial partner, who he would not name. Green said he…

summary: Moose that drop dead are responsibility of homeowner The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (April 19, 9:40 am ADT) - Eagle River resident Glenn Gibeault discovered a dead moose on his quarter-acre near Eagle River Road last month. As the snow started melting, an ear emerged. He called the state Department of Fish and Game. "'It's your moose,'" Gibeault said he was told. If a moose dies alongside a road or in a ditch, Fish and Game will call on a trapper or city or state road crews to haul it off. But if one dies on private property, the homeowner has to dispose of the carcass. "Our policy is once a moose is dead, it's not ours anymore. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: An amendment to the bill set a 50 percent, simple majority threshold for voter support for any new or changed city sales taxes. That would overturn an Anchorage city requirement that a 60 percent supermajority is needed to enact a sales tax, essentially making it easier to pass a sales tax. Anchorage voters adopted the 60 percent rule in 1997. Assembly Chairman Dick Traini said he objects to the amendment because it tries to run around what voters decided. "It's a rip-off for Anchorage taxpayers," he said. "If we could have a local sales tax that offset property taxes that would be OK. summary: Wigen's body was found on April 1, 2003, buried about 50 feet from the green cabin where she lived, up the hill from the center of Tenakee Springs. summary: DESCRIPTION: A transportation, communications and supply hub for the Interior. A bit more than half the population is Alaska Native. McGrath offers a variety of employment opportunities and a diverse cash economy, but subsistence remains an important part of the local culture; salmon, moose, caribou, bear and rabbits are utilized. Some residents trap and tend vegetable gardens. Some families enter dog teams in the Iditarod, Kuskokwim 300 and Mail Trail 200 sled dog races. There are two schools, attended by about 180 students. HISTORY: McGrath was a seasonal Upper Kuskokwim Athabascan village used as a meeting and trading place for Big River, Nikolai, Telida and Lake Minchumina residents. summary: There are people in this city who foolishly think skiing peaks with the Tour of Anchorage in the first week of March. Some of these people are my friends. And I must confess that there was a time when the warm sun of March and April turned my thoughts to fishing and mountain biking on snow-free trails. Then came a sad winter of little snow when skiing was lousy during January and February and on into March. By then, it was easy to think that spring would come early to Southcentral Alaska, that the fishing that doesn't begin until May might actually begin in April. This was not to be, of course. title: Klukwan group moves ahead with herbicide spraying plans
summary: A Native village corporation is moving ahead with controversial plans to spray herbicides this summer on its brushy forest land in Southeast Alaska. Klukwan Inc. of Haines has asked for a state permit to spray Accord and Arsenal by helicopter on 2,000 acres on Long Island, near the community of Hydaburg in southern Southeast. The permit application, submitted last month, follows new state regulations that allow aerial herbicide spraying for forestry purposes, improving Klukwan's chances of getting a permit. Klukwan's president, Tom Crandall, declined to comment. The company's chief forester, Jim Tuttle, could not be reached.
